title: UEFI/BIOS Firmware Rootkit Detection Guide
content:
# UEFI/BIOS Firmware Rootkit Detection Guide

## Overview

Firmware rootkits are among the most persistent and difficult-to-detect threats in cybersecurity. By compromising UEFI (Unified Extensible Firmware Interface) or legacy BIOS firmware, attackers achieve persistence that survives operating system reinstallation, hard drive replacement, and most security tools. Firmware-level threats operate below the operating system, giving attackers control before any security software loads.

Notable firmware threats include LoJax (first in-the-wild UEFI rootkit, attributed to APT28), MosaicRegressor, ESPecter, FinSpy UEFI bootkit, CosmicStrand, and BlackLotus (first in-the-wild bootkit to bypass UEFI Secure Boot on fully patched Windows 11).

SOC analysts should understand firmware attack surfaces, detection capabilities, and response procedures for suspected firmware compromise.

## Firmware Attack Surface

| Component | Description | Attack Vector |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| SPI Flash | Stores UEFI firmware image | Direct write via vulnerability or physical access |
| UEFI DXE Drivers | Loaded during boot, before OS | Malicious DXE driver injection into firmware image |
| UEFI Runtime Services | Services available to OS after boot | Compromised runtime services for persistent access |
| EFI System Partition (ESP) | FAT32 partition with boot loaders | Modification of bootloader or addition of malicious EFI executables |
| NVRAM Variables | Non-volatile storage for UEFI settings | Manipulation of boot order, Secure Boot settings |
| Option ROMs | Firmware on expansion cards (NIC, GPU) | Malicious code in PCI device firmware |
| Intel ME / AMD PSP | Management engine running independently | Exploitation of management engine for persistent access |
| SMM (System Management Mode) | Privileged CPU mode for firmware | SMM-based rootkits operating in ring -2 |

## Firmware Threat Categories

### UEFI Rootkits (SPI Flash Modification)

| Threat | Year | Actor | Technique |
|--------|------|-------|-----------|
| LoJax | 2018 | APT28 (Fancy Bear) | Modified SPI flash to add malicious UEFI DXE driver |
| MosaicRegressor | 2020 | Unknown (Chinese-speaking) | Modified UEFI firmware with multiple malicious DXE drivers |
| CosmicStrand | 2022 | Unknown (Chinese-speaking) | Firmware rootkit in motherboard UEFI, survived for years |

### UEFI Bootkits (ESP Modification)

| Threat | Year | Actor | Technique |
|--------|------|-------|-----------|
| FinSpy | 2021 | FinFisher (commercial) | Replaced Windows Boot Manager with trojanized version |
| ESPecter | 2021 | Unknown | Modified Windows Boot Manager on ESP |
| BlackLotus | 2023 | Criminal marketplace | Bypassed Secure Boot via CVE-2022-21894, self-installs from Windows |

## Detection Methods

### Method 1: UEFI Firmware Integrity Verification

**CHIPSEC Framework (Intel)**:

CHIPSEC is an open-source framework for analyzing platform security, including UEFI firmware integrity.

```bash
# Install CHIPSEC (requires kernel driver)
pip install chipsec

# Verify SPI flash write protection
sudo python -m chipsec_main -m common.spi_lock
# PASS: SPI flash is write-protected
# FAIL: SPI flash is writable (vulnerable to firmware modification)

# Check BIOS write enable (BLE) bit
sudo python -m chipsec_main -m common.bios_wp
# Verifies BIOS write protection is enabled

# Check Secure Boot configuration
sudo python -m chipsec_main -m common.secureboot.variables
# Lists Secure Boot variables and their status

# Dump SPI flash for analysis
sudo python -m chipsec_util spi dump firmware.bin
# Creates binary dump of the entire SPI flash chip

# Verify firmware integrity against known-good image
sudo python -m chipsec_main -m tools.uefi.whitelist -a generate,whitelist.json,firmware_known_good.bin
sudo python -m chipsec_main -m tools.uefi.whitelist -a check,whitelist.json,firmware_current.bin
```

**Key CHIPSEC checks**:

| Check | Module | What It Detects |
|-------|--------|-----------------|
| SPI Flash Lock | common.spi_lock | Whether SPI flash can be written to (modification vulnerability) |
| BIOS Write Protection | common.bios_wp | Whether BIOS region is write-protected |
| Secure Boot Variables | common.secureboot.variables | Secure Boot state and key enrollment |
| SMM BIOS Write Protection | common.bios_smi | Whether SMI handler protects BIOS writes |
| UEFI Whitelist | tools.uefi.whitelist | Compares firmware modules against known-good baseline |
| S3 Boot Script | common.uefi.s3bootscript | Checks for S3 resume script vulnerabilities |

### Method 2: Secure Boot Verification

**Windows**:
```powershell
# Check Secure Boot status
Confirm-SecureBootUEFI
# Returns True if Secure Boot is enabled and enforcing

# Check Secure Boot policy
Get-SecureBootPolicy

# List Secure Boot certificates (PK, KEK, db, dbx)
Get-SecureBootUEFI -Name PK
Get-SecureBootUEFI -Name KEK
Get-SecureBootUEFI -Name db
Get-SecureBootUEFI -Name dbx

# Check if Secure Boot was recently modified
# Look in System event log for Secure Boot changes
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='System'; Id=1032} | Select-Object TimeCreated, Message

# Verify boot integrity with Measured Boot
# Check TPM PCR values (Platform Configuration Registers)
tpm2_pcrread sha256:0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7
```

**Linux**:
```bash
# Check Secure Boot status
mokutil --sb-state
# SecureBoot enabled/disabled

# List enrolled keys
mokutil --list-enrolled

# Check EFI variables
efivar -l | grep SecureBoot
efivar -d -n 8be4df61-93ca-11d2-aa0d-00e098032b8c-SecureBoot

# Check TPM PCR values
tpm2_pcrread sha256:0+1+2+3+4+5+6+7

# Verify boot chain
sbverify --cert /path/to/cert.pem /boot/efi/EFI/BOOT/BOOTX64.EFI
```

### Method 3: EFI System Partition (ESP) Inspection

```bash
# Mount and inspect the ESP
# Windows:
mountvol S: /S
dir /s S:\EFI\

# Linux:
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt/efi
find /mnt/efi -type f -exec sha256sum {} \;

# Check for unexpected EFI executables
find /mnt/efi -name "*.efi" -exec file {} \;

# Compare boot loader hashes against known-good values
sha256sum /mnt/efi/EFI/Microsoft/Boot/bootmgfw.efi
sha256sum /mnt/efi/EFI/BOOT/BOOTX64.EFI
# Compare with hashes from a known-clean system with the same OS version

# Check for hidden files on ESP
find /mnt/efi -name ".*" -type f

# Check modification timestamps
find /mnt/efi -type f -printf '%T+ %p\n' | sort

# Look for BlackLotus indicators
# BlackLotus creates files in ESP:\EFI\Microsoft\Boot\
# Look for unexpected .efi files or modified grubx64.efi
find /mnt/efi -name "*.efi" -newer /mnt/efi/EFI/Microsoft/Boot/bootmgfw.efi
```

### Method 4: Runtime Integrity Monitoring

**Windows Defender System Guard (Windows 10/11)**:
- Uses VBS (Virtualization-Based Security) to protect firmware measurement
- Attestation of boot integrity through TPM
- Reports firmware integrity status through Windows Security Center

```powershell
# Check System Guard status
Get-MpComputerStatus | Select-Object *SystemGuard*, *Firmware*

# Check VBS status
Get-CimInstance -ClassName Win32_DeviceGuard -Namespace root\Microsoft\Windows\DeviceGuard

# Check HVCI (Hypervisor-Enforced Code Integrity)
Get-ProcessMitigation -System | Select-Object HVCI
```

**Linux Runtime Verification**:
```bash
# Check for unsigned kernel modules
for mod in $(lsmod | awk 'NR>1 {print $1}'); do
  info=$(modinfo "$mod" 2>/dev/null)
  sig=$(echo "$info" | grep "sig_id")
  if [ -z "$sig" ]; then
    echo "UNSIGNED: $mod"
  fi
done

# Check kernel lockdown mode
cat /sys/kernel/security/lockdown

# Verify kernel image integrity
sha256sum /boot/vmlinuz-$(uname -r)
# Compare with package manager expected hash
```

## Indicators of Firmware Compromise

| Indicator | Severity | Description |
|-----------|----------|-------------|
| SPI flash write protection disabled | Critical | Firmware can be modified by software |
| Secure Boot disabled unexpectedly | Critical | Boot chain integrity not enforced |
| Unknown UEFI DXE drivers | Critical | Possible rootkit module in firmware |
| Modified boot loader hash | Critical | Bootkit likely installed |
| Unexpected EFI executables on ESP | High | Possible bootkit or persistence mechanism |
| TPM PCR value mismatch | High | Boot chain has been modified |
| SMM handler modifications | Critical | Ring -2 rootkit |
| Firmware version mismatch | Medium | Firmware may have been downgraded or replaced |
| Unusual boot time increase | Low | Additional firmware modules loading |
| Secure Boot key enrollment changes | High | Attacker-controlled keys allow malicious code signing |

## Response Procedures

### Suspected Firmware Compromise Response

1. **Isolation**
   - Disconnect the system from the network immediately
   - Do not power off (preserve volatile evidence first)
   - Acquire memory image before any other action

2. **Evidence Collection**
   - Dump SPI flash using CHIPSEC or hardware programmer
   - Capture ESP contents with hashes
   - Record Secure Boot variable state
   - Export TPM PCR values
   - Collect UEFI event logs
   - Document firmware version from BIOS setup

3. **Analysis**
   - Compare firmware dump against known-good baseline
   - Extract and analyze individual UEFI modules (UEFITool)
   - Scan EFI executables on ESP with AV/EDR
   - Check boot loader signatures
   - Analyze any unknown DXE drivers

4. **Remediation**
   - **If SPI flash compromised**: Reflash firmware from known-good image using hardware programmer (software reflash may not be trustworthy)
   - **If ESP compromised**: Clean and rebuild ESP from installation media
   - **If Secure Boot keys compromised**: Reset Secure Boot to factory keys
   - **In all cases**: Replace the system if reflash cannot be verified

5. **Hardening**
   - Enable and verify SPI flash write protection
   - Enable Secure Boot with custom keys if possible
   - Enable UEFI firmware password
   - Enable TPM and Measured Boot
   - Deploy firmware integrity monitoring solution
   - Update firmware to latest version from manufacturer

### Firmware Remediation Decision

```
Firmware compromise confirmed
  |
  +-- SPI flash modified?
  |   |
  |   +-- YES --> Hardware reflash required
  |   |           Use external SPI programmer
  |   |           Flash known-good image from manufacturer
  |   |           Verify after flash with CHIPSEC
  |   |           If reflash cannot be verified --> Replace hardware
  |   |
  |   +-- NO --> ESP/bootloader modified only?
  |       |
  |       +-- YES --> Rebuild ESP from installation media
  |       |           Re-enroll Secure Boot keys
  |       |           Verify boot chain integrity
  |       |
  |       +-- NO --> Investigate further
  |
  +-- After remediation:
      +-- Monitor system for recurrence
      +-- Investigate initial access vector
      +-- Check all systems with same hardware/firmware version
```

## Prevention and Hardening

| Control | Description | Priority |
|---------|-------------|----------|
| Enable Secure Boot | Verify boot chain signatures | Critical |
| Enable SPI flash write protection | Prevent software-based firmware modification | Critical |
| UEFI firmware password | Prevent unauthorized BIOS/UEFI setting changes | High |
| TPM + Measured Boot | Cryptographic verification of boot components | High |
| Firmware update policy | Keep firmware current, apply security patches | High |
| VBS / System Guard | Hardware-backed boot integrity (Windows) | High |
| UEFI Capsule Update enforcement | Only accept signed firmware updates | Medium |
| Physical security | Prevent hardware-level firmware access | Medium |
| Boot order lock | Prevent unauthorized boot device changes | Medium |

## MITRE ATT&CK Mapping

| Technique | ID | Description |
|-----------|----|-----------|
| Pre-OS Boot: System Firmware | T1542.001 | Modification of system firmware (BIOS/UEFI) |
| Pre-OS Boot: Component Firmware | T1542.002 | Modification of component firmware (NIC, GPU) |
| Pre-OS Boot: Bootkit | T1542.003 | Modification of boot process (MBR/VBR/EFI) |
| Subvert Trust Controls: Code Signing | T1553.006 | Abuse of Secure Boot bypasses |
| Exploitation of Pre-OS Boot | T1542 | General pre-OS boot exploitation |

## Tools Reference

| Tool | Purpose | Source |
|------|---------|--------|
| CHIPSEC | Platform security assessment | https://github.com/chipsec/chipsec |
| UEFITool | UEFI firmware image parser and editor | https://github.com/LongSoft/UEFITool |
| UEFI Firmware Parser | Extract and analyze UEFI firmware | https://github.com/theopolis/uefi-firmware-parser |
| fwupd | Linux firmware update daemon | https://fwupd.org/ |
| FirmWalker | Firmware analysis search tool | https://github.com/craigz28/firmwalker |
| Binwalk | Firmware analysis and extraction | https://github.com/ReFirmLabs/binwalk |

## References

- ESET Research: LoJax (First UEFI rootkit found in the wild)
- Kaspersky: MosaicRegressor and CosmicStrand analysis
- ESET Research: BlackLotus UEFI Bootkit analysis
- NIST SP 800-147: BIOS Protection Guidelines
- NIST SP 800-147B: Server BIOS Protection Guidelines
- NIST SP 800-155: BIOS Integrity Measurement Guidelines
- MITRE ATT&CK Pre-OS Boot: https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1542/
